1	import { NextRequest } from 'next/server'
2	import { ZEO_VERSION, ZEO_RELEASE_DATE } from '@/lib/constants'
3	
4	interface UpdateRequest {
5	  platform: string
6	  arch: string
7	  current_version: string
8	  lang: string
9	  last_check: string
10	}
11	
12	/**
13	 * Compare two semver strings. Returns:
14	 *  -1 if a < b, 0 if a == b, 1 if a > b
15	 */
16	function compareSemver(a: string, b: string): number {
17	  // Strip leading 'v' if present
18	  const cleanA = a.replace(/^v/, '')
19	  const cleanB = b.replace(/^v/, '')
20	
21	  const partsA = cleanA.split(/[.\-]/).map(p => {
22	    const n = parseInt(p, 10)
23	    return isNaN(n) ? 0 : n
24	  })
25	  const partsB = cleanB.split(/[.\-]/).map(p => {
26	    const n = parseInt(p, 10)
27	    return isNaN(n) ? 0 : n
28	  })
29	
30	  const len = Math.max(partsA.length, partsB.length)
31	  for (let i = 0; i < len; i++) {
32	    const numA = partsA[i] ?? 0
33	    const numB = partsB[i] ?? 0
34	    if (numA < numB) return -1
35	    if (numA > numB) return 1
36	  }
37	  return 0
38	}
39	
40	export async function POST(request: NextRequest) {
41	  let body: UpdateRequest
42	  try {
43	    body = await request.json()
44	  } catch {
45	    return Response.json({ error: 'Invalid JSON body' }, { status: 400 })
46	  }
47	
48	  const { current_version } = body
49	  if (!current_version || typeof current_version !== 'string') {
50	    return Response.json({ error: 'current_version is required' }, { status: 400 })
51	  }
52	
53	  // If the client version is behind the latest, return update info
54	  if (compareSemver(current_version, ZEO_VERSION) < 0) {
55	    const baseUrl = `${request.nextUrl.protocol}//${request.nextUrl.host}`
56	    return Response.json({
57	      version: ZEO_VERSION,
58	      release_date: ZEO_RELEASE_DATE,
59	      details_url: `${baseUrl}/docs/changelog`,
60	      downloads_url: `${baseUrl}/download`,
61	    })
62	  }
63	
64	  // No update available
65	  return new Response(null, { status: 204 })
66	}

content.text:
**Bug found and it's much simpler than file-vs-storage logic — there is no storage logic.**

`web/app/api/v1/update/route.ts:54` compares the client's `current_version` against a **hardcoded constant** in `web/lib/constants.ts:2`:

```ts
export const ZEO_VERSION = '0.1.1'
export const ZEO_RELEASE_DATE = '2026-03-27'
```

So when v0.1.1 asks "is there anything newer?", the server compares `0.1.1 < 0.1.1` → false → returns 204. The server **never reads your zeo-releases storage** — uploading new files there has zero effect on the API response. The constant is the single source of truth.

This explains everything you saw: your 0.2.0 uploads are real, but the API doesn't know about them.

## To fix today's test (one-line edit + deploy)

Bump the constant in `web/lib/constants.ts`:
```ts
export const ZEO_VERSION = '0.2.0'
export const ZEO_RELEASE_DATE = '2026-05-18'  // or whenever 0.2.0 shipped
```
Deploy the web app. Curl will then return 200 with `{version:"0.2.0", …}`, and v0.1.1 launcher will pop the update dialog.

## Longer-term — this design is fragile

Every release requires a coordinated PR to bump `ZEO_VERSION` + redeploy the web app, *and* uploading the artifacts to storage. Forget either step and:
- Forget the constant bump → silent no-op for all users (what just happened to you).
- Forget the storage upload → users see the dialog, click Download, and 404.

Two saner options:

1. **Derive `ZEO_VERSION` from storage at request time** — list the `zeo-releases/` folder, take the highest semver name, cache for ~60s. One source of truth, but adds a storage list to every update check.
2. **Use a small manifest file** like `zeo-releases/latest.json` (`{version, release_date, downloads_url}`) that gets written as part of the same upload step. The route just fetches and returns that file. One round-trip, atomic with the artifact upload if the upload step writes them together.

Option 2 is the standard pattern (matches how Sparkle/electron-updater feeds work) and is what I'd recommend.

I can apply the one-line constant bump and/or implement option 2 — which would you like? Note the web repo is a separate codebase (`../web`), so anything I change there is a separate edit/commit from the Zeo C++ work we've been doing.

content.content:
1	# CLAUDE.md - Zeo.dev Codebase Guide
2	
3	## Project Overview
4	
5	**Zeo** is an open source fork of KiCad with a focus on automation and AI, consisting of:
6	
7	1. **Web Application**: Next.js-based landing page, authentication, and usage dashboard
8	2. **LLM API Proxy**: Server-side endpoint that proxies Claude API requests with added features
9	3. **Context Management**: Automatic context compaction and conversation summarization
10	4. **Usage Tracking**: Per-user API usage metering and quota management via Supabase
11	
12	The application serves as the authentication and billing backend for the Zeo KiCad agent, which runs locally and communicates with the API proxy.
13	
14	## Tech Stack
15	
16	| Technology | Version | Purpose |
17	|------------|---------|---------|
18	| Next.js | 16.1.4 | React framework with App Router |
19	| React | 19.2.3 | UI library |
20	| TypeScript | ^5 | Type safety (strict mode enabled) |
21	| Supabase | ^2.45.0 | Auth, database, RLS |
22	| Tailwind CSS | ^4 | Styling |
23	| Framer Motion | ^12.27.5 | Animations |
24	| Vercel Functions | ^3.3.6 | Serverless deployment |
25	
26	## Directory Structure
27	
28	```
29	zeo.dev/
30	├── app/                          # Next.js App Router
31	│   ├── api/
32	│   │   └── llm/messages/route.ts # Main LLM proxy endpoint (POST /api/llm/messages)
33	│   ├── auth/
34	│   │   ├── callback/route.ts     # OAuth callback handler
35	│   │   ├── signin/page.tsx       # Sign-in page with OAuth UI
36	│   │   ├── error/page.tsx        # Auth error page
37	│   │   └── components/AuthUI.tsx # Supabase Auth UI component
38	│   ├── dashboard/
39	│   │   ├── page.tsx              # Usage dashboard (server component)
40	│   │   └── components/SignOutButton.tsx
41	│   ├── components/
42	│   │   ├── MagnifyingText.tsx    # Animated text effect for landing
43	│   │   ├── AnimatedButton.tsx    # Hover-animated button component
44	│   │   └── ConnectDotsGame.tsx   # Interactive footer game
45	│   ├── fonts/Bitcount/           # Custom variable font
46	│   ├── globals.css               # Global styles + Tailwind
47	│   ├── layout.tsx                # Root layout with font loading
48	│   └── page.tsx                  # Landing page
49	├── lib/
50	│   ├── supabase.ts               # Browser Supabase client

73	
74	The core backend endpoint that:
75	- Validates Bearer token against Supabase auth
76	- Restricts to allowed Claude models (`claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929`, `claude-opus-4-5-20251101`)
77	- Injects system prompt server-side, with optional plan-mode addendum based on `agent_mode` metadata

91	- `summarizeConversation()`: Uses Claude Haiku for efficient summarization
92	- Creates summary + acknowledgment messages for seamless continuation

94	### Database Schema
95	
96	The canonical schema lives in `supabase/schema.sql`. Update that file when the schema changes instead of creating new migration files. When schema changes are needed, provide the migration SQL to the user in chat so they can run it manually.
97	
98	**`api_usage` table:** Tracks per-request LLM usage (tokens, cost, model, web search/fetch counts).
99	
100	**`user_quotas` table:** Per-user billing state — spending limit (`spending_limit_cents`, nullable for no limit), Stripe IDs, plan, and free credits.
101	
102	## Development Commands
103	
104	```bash
105	npm run dev      # Start development server
106	npm run build    # Production build
107	npm run start    # Start production server
108	npm run lint     # Run ESLint
109	npx tsc          # Type check without emitting
110	```

112	**Always validate changes** by running `npm run build`, `npm run lint`, and `npm run test:run` before considering any task complete. These commands must pass without errors.

117	```
118	NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL=
119	NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=
120	SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY=
121	ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=
122	```

124	## Code Conventions
125	
126	### TypeScript
127	- Strict mode enabled

131	### Styling
132	- Modern dark theme with deep blacks and clean whites via CSS variables
133	- Custom color classes: `bg-bg-hard`, `bg-bg`, `bg-bg1`, `text-fg`, `text-fg2`, `text-fg4`
134	- Accent colors: `text-blue`, `text-cyan`, `bg-green`, `bg-yellow`, `bg-red`

136	- Bitcount variable font for headings

140	- Use `"use client"` directive for client components
141	- Server components preferred for data fetching (dashboard)
142	- Framer Motion for animations
143	- Tailwind for all styling (no CSS modules)
144	
145	### API Routes
146	- Use Next.js Route Handlers (`route.ts`)
147	- Validate auth via Supabase service role
148	- Stream responses where appropriate
149	- Use `waitUntil()` for background tasks on Vercel
150	
151	## Important Patterns
152	
153	### Authentication Flow
154	1. User signs in via OAuth (Google/GitHub) on `/auth/signin`
155	2. Callback redirects to `/auth/callback` which exchanges code for session
156	3. Authenticated users can access `/dashboard`
157	4. API requests use Bearer token from Supabase session
158	
159	### LLM Request Flow
160	1. Client sends POST to `/api/llm/messages` with Bearer token
161	2. Server validates token, injects system prompt (with plan-mode addendum if `agent_mode` is `"plan"`)
162	3. Adds cache markers, context management config, and thinking config
163	4. Forwards to Anthropic API with beta headers
164	5. Streams response back, tracking tokens
165	6. Records usage to database asynchronously
166	
167	### Context Compaction
168	When input tokens exceed 175K:
169	1. Anthropic's context management clears old tool uses
170	2. If still over limit, server summarizes older messages
171	3. Returns `context_exhausted` error with summarized messages
172	4. Client retries with compacted history
173	
174	## Testing
175	
176	```bash
177	npm run test         # Run vitest in watch mode
178	npm run test:run     # Run unit tests once
179	npm run test:integration  # Run integration tests
180	npm run test:e2e     # Run Playwright E2E tests
181	npm run test:all     # Run all tests (unit, integration, e2e)
182	```
183	
184	- Unit/integration tests use Vitest with happy-dom
185	- E2E tests use Playwright
186	- MSW for mocking API calls
187	- **Always use imported constants in tests** - never hardcode values that are defined in constants files (e.g., import `THINKING_EFFORT` from `constants.ts` rather than using `'max'` directly)
188	
189	## Deployment
190	
191	Deployed on Vercel:
192	- Automatic deployments from main branch
193	- Environment variables configured in Vercel dashboard
194	- Uses Vercel Functions for API routes
195	- `waitUntil()` for non-blocking database writes
196	
197	## Common Tasks
198	
199	### Adding a New Agent Mode
200	1. Create prompt file in `lib/prompts/` with exported constant
201	2. Export from `lib/prompts/index.ts`
202	3. Add mode handling in `route.ts` where `agent_mode` is checked
203	
204	### Modifying Usage Tracking
205	1. Update `supabase/schema.sql` and provide migration SQL in chat
206	2. Modify cost calculation in `route.ts`
207	3. Update dashboard queries in `dashboard/page.tsx`
208	
209	### Adding New Claude Models
210	1. Add model ID to `ALLOWED_MODELS` array in `route.ts`
211	2. Add cost per 1M tokens to `MODEL_COSTS` object
212	
213	## Security Considerations
214	
215	- Never expose `SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY` to client
216	- API routes validate Bearer token before processing
217	- RLS policies restrict data access per user
218	- Sensitive files (`.env*`) are gitignored
219	- OAuth providers (Google, GitHub) handle credential storage
220	
221	## Known Limitations
222	
223	- No rate limiting beyond Supabase/Anthropic defaults
224	- Summarization uses Haiku which may lose some context fidelity
225	- Context management relies on Anthropic beta features
226	- No offline support for the web dashboard

file.content:
# CLAUDE.md - Zeo.dev Codebase Guide

## Project Overview

**Zeo** is an open source fork of KiCad with a focus on automation and AI, consisting of:

1. **Web Application**: Next.js-based landing page, authentication, and usage dashboard
2. **LLM API Proxy**: Server-side endpoint that proxies Claude API requests with added features
3. **Context Management**: Automatic context compaction and conversation summarization
4. **Usage Tracking**: Per-user API usage metering and quota management via Supabase

The application serves as the authentication and billing backend for the Zeo KiCad agent, which runs locally and communicates with the API proxy.

## Tech Stack

| Technology | Version | Purpose |
|------------|---------|---------|
| Next.js | 16.1.4 | React framework with App Router |
| React | 19.2.3 | UI library |
| TypeScript | ^5 | Type safety (strict mode enabled) |
| Supabase | ^2.45.0 | Auth, database, RLS |
| Tailwind CSS | ^4 | Styling |
| Framer Motion | ^12.27.5 | Animations |
| Vercel Functions | ^3.3.6 | Serverless deployment |

## Directory Structure

```
zeo.dev/
├── app/                          # Next.js App Router
│   ├── api/
│   │   └── llm/messages/route.ts # Main LLM proxy endpoint (POST /api/llm/messages)
│   ├── auth/
│   │   ├── callback/route.ts     # OAuth callback handler
│   │   ├── signin/page.tsx       # Sign-in page with OAuth UI
│   │   ├── error/page.tsx        # Auth error page
│   │   └── components/AuthUI.tsx # Supabase Auth UI component
│   ├── dashboard/
│   │   ├── page.tsx              # Usage dashboard (server component)
│   │   └── components/SignOutButton.tsx
│   ├── components/
│   │   ├── MagnifyingText.tsx    # Animated text effect for landing
│   │   ├── AnimatedButton.tsx    # Hover-animated button component
│   │   └── ConnectDotsGame.tsx   # Interactive footer game
│   ├── fonts/Bitcount/           # Custom variable font
│   ├── globals.css               # Global styles + Tailwind
│   ├── layout.tsx                # Root layout with font loading
│   └── page.tsx                  # Landing page
├── lib/
│   ├── supabase.ts               # Browser Supabase client
│   ├── supabase-server.ts        # Server-side Supabase client (SSR)
│   ├── prompts/
│   │   ├── index.ts              # Prompt exports (SYSTEM_PROMPT, PLAN_MODE_ADDENDUM)
│   │   ├── system-prompt.ts      # Core system prompt for the KiCad agent
│   │   └── plan-mode-addendum.ts # Read-only planning mode addendum
│   └── context-recovery/
│       ├── index.ts              # Module exports
│       ├── types.ts              # TypeScript interfaces
│       ├── message-splitter.ts   # Smart message splitting (preserves tool pairs)
│       ├── summarizer.ts         # Conversation summarization via Haiku
│       └── prompts.ts            # Summarization prompt template
├── supabase/
│   └── schema.sql                # Database schema (source of truth, not runnable)
├── proxy.ts                      # Supabase session refresh proxy
├── package.json
├── tsconfig.json                 # TypeScript config (strict, @/* path alias)
└── eslint.config.mjs             # ESLint with Next.js rules
```

## Key Components

### LLM API Proxy (`app/api/llm/messages/route.ts`)

The core backend endpoint that:
- Validates Bearer token against Supabase auth
- Restricts to allowed Claude models (`claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929`, `claude-opus-4-5-20251101`)
- Injects system prompt server-side, with optional plan-mode addendum based on `agent_mode` metadata
- Adds prompt caching markers for efficiency
- Configures context management with `clear_tool_uses_20250919`
- Enables extended thinking with 16K token budget
- Streams responses while tracking usage
- Records usage to `api_usage` table with cost calculation
- Handles context exhaustion via automatic summarization

**Configuration constants**: See `app/api/llm/constants.ts` for token thresholds, thinking effort, model costs, etc.

### Context Recovery (`lib/context-recovery/`)

Handles conversation summarization when context limits are approached:
- `splitMessages()`: Splits messages while preserving tool_use/tool_result pairs
- `summarizeConversation()`: Uses Claude Haiku for efficient summarization
- Creates summary + acknowledgment messages for seamless continuation

### Database Schema

The canonical schema lives in `supabase/schema.sql`. Update that file when the schema changes instead of creating new migration files. When schema changes are needed, provide the migration SQL to the user in chat so they can run it manually.

**`api_usage` table:** Tracks per-request LLM usage (tokens, cost, model, web search/fetch counts).

**`user_quotas` table:** Per-user billing state — spending limit (`spending_limit_cents`, nullable for no limit), Stripe IDs, plan, and free credits.

## Development Commands

```bash
npm run dev      # Start development server
npm run build    # Production build
npm run start    # Start production server
npm run lint     # Run ESLint
npx tsc          # Type check without emitting
```

**Always validate changes** by running `npm run build`, `npm run lint`, and `npm run test:run` before considering any task complete. These commands must pass without errors.

## Environment Variables

Required in `.env.local`:
```
NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL=
NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=
SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY=
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=
```

## Code Conventions

### TypeScript
- Strict mode enabled
- Use `@/*` path alias for imports from project root
- Prefer explicit types over `any`

### Styling
- Modern dark theme with deep blacks and clean whites via CSS variables
- Custom color classes: `bg-bg-hard`, `bg-bg`, `bg-bg1`, `text-fg`, `text-fg2`, `text-fg4`
- Accent colors: `text-blue`, `text-cyan`, `bg-green`, `bg-yellow`, `bg-red`
- Zeo brand colors: `zeo-primary` (blue), `zeo-accent` (cyan)
- Bitcount variable font for headings
- Monospace font (`font-mono`) throughout UI

### Components
- Use `"use client"` directive for client components
- Server components preferred for data fetching (dashboard)
- Framer Motion for animations
- Tailwind for all styling (no CSS modules)

### API Routes
- Use Next.js Route Handlers (`route.ts`)
- Validate auth via Supabase service role
- Stream responses where appropriate
- Use `waitUntil()` for background tasks on Vercel

## Important Patterns

### Authentication Flow
1. User signs in via OAuth (Google/GitHub) on `/auth/signin`
2. Callback redirects to `/auth/callback` which exchanges code for session
3. Authenticated users can access `/dashboard`
4. API requests use Bearer token from Supabase session

### LLM Request Flow
1. Client sends POST to `/api/llm/messages` with Bearer token
2. Server validates token, injects system prompt (with plan-mode addendum if `agent_mode` is `"plan"`)
3. Adds cache markers, context management config, and thinking config
4. Forwards to Anthropic API with beta headers
5. Streams response back, tracking tokens
6. Records usage to database asynchronously

### Context Compaction
When input tokens exceed 175K:
1. Anthropic's context management clears old tool uses
2. If still over limit, server summarizes older messages
3. Returns `context_exhausted` error with summarized messages
4. Client retries with compacted history

## Testing

```bash
npm run test         # Run vitest in watch mode
npm run test:run     # Run unit tests once
npm run test:integration  # Run integration tests
npm run test:e2e     # Run Playwright E2E tests
npm run test:all     # Run all tests (unit, integration, e2e)
```

- Unit/integration tests use Vitest with happy-dom
- E2E tests use Playwright
- MSW for mocking API calls
- **Always use imported constants in tests** - never hardcode values that are defined in constants files (e.g., import `THINKING_EFFORT` from `constants.ts` rather than using `'max'` directly)

## Deployment

Deployed on Vercel:
- Automatic deployments from main branch
- Environment variables configured in Vercel dashboard
- Uses Vercel Functions for API routes
- `waitUntil()` for non-blocking database writes

## Common Tasks

### Adding a New Agent Mode
1. Create prompt file in `lib/prompts/` with exported constant
2. Export from `lib/prompts/index.ts`
3. Add mode handling in `route.ts` where `agent_mode` is checked

### Modifying Usage Tracking
1. Update `supabase/schema.sql` and provide migration SQL in chat
2. Modify cost calculation in `route.ts`
3. Update dashboard queries in `dashboard/page.tsx`

### Adding New Claude Models
1. Add model ID to `ALLOWED_MODELS` array in `route.ts`
2. Add cost per 1M tokens to `MODEL_COSTS` object

## Security Considerations

- Never expose `SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY` to client
- API routes validate Bearer token before processing
- RLS policies restrict data access per user
- Sensitive files (`.env*`) are gitignored
- OAuth providers (Google, GitHub) handle credential storage

## Known Limitations

- No rate limiting beyond Supabase/Anthropic defaults
- Summarization uses Haiku which may lose some context fidelity
- Context management relies on Anthropic beta features
- No offline support for the web dashboard

aiTitle: Review Zeo update system notification flow

input.description: Find references to zeo-releases in web code

content.text:
Before I implement, one detail worth confirming. I see `lib/downloads.ts:35-73` already has `listAvailableVersions()` that enumerates the `zeo-releases` bucket, validates which platform files are present, and returns the list sorted descending. That same code is presumably what feeds the `/download` page.

That changes the calculus a bit: option 1 ("derive from storage") is essentially **free** here because the function already exists. Option 2 (manifest file) adds a new artifact you have to remember to write on every release.

Let me confirm scope before I touch anything.

questions.question: Given listAvailableVersions() already exists, how would you like the update route to determine the latest version?

options.label: Reuse listAvailableVersions() (Recommended)
options.description: Update route calls the existing function, takes the highest semver entry that has a download for the requesting platform, returns that. No new artifact to maintain — the storage upload IS the source of truth. Single read of storage per check (Supabase list is fast; can add a 60s revalidate cache).

options.label: Write a latest.json manifest
options.description: Keep your original option 2 — a small JSON file at zeo-releases/latest.json that the route fetches. You'll need a script or admin step that writes it whenever you upload a new release. More moving parts, but the route does a single object fetch instead of a list.

options.label: Both — manifest with storage-list fallback
options.description: Route tries latest.json first; if missing or stale, falls back to listAvailableVersions(). Safest but most code to maintain.

questions.question: Should the chosen version also be filtered by the requesting platform?

options.label: Yes, platform-aware (Recommended)
options.description: If the requester is on macOS and the latest version has no .dmg yet (e.g., 0.2.0 uploaded for Linux only), return the next-newest version that DOES have a macOS file. Avoids advertising an update the user can't download.

options.label: No, always return absolute latest
options.description: Simpler logic, but if a partial upload happens (only Linux artifact present), Mac users will see the popup and 404 on download.

content.text:
Build passes. The `/api/v1/update` route is listed as a function in the output and compiles cleanly. Type-check and Next.js build both clean; the lint and test failures already existed in unrelated files.

## What changed

**`web/app/api/v1/update/route.ts`** — rewritten:

- No longer imports or compares against the hardcoded `ZEO_VERSION` constant.
- Now calls `listAvailableVersions()` (already in `lib/downloads.ts`) on each request.
- Maps the launcher's platform string (`"macos"` / `"windows"` / `"linux"`) to `lib/downloads.ts`'s Platform type via a small alias table — including a defensive fallback to 204 for unknown platforms so old/exotic clients fail closed.
- Filters to versions that have an artifact for the requesting platform, takes the highest, compares against `current_version` using the existing `compareSemver`.
- Returns the same response shape as before (`version`, `release_date`, `details_url`, `downloads_url`). `release_date` is now an empty string since the listing doesn't have that metadata — the launcher's C++ struct accepts it (the dialog never displayed it anyway, so no client-side change needed).
- 204 on: no platform match, no versions with this platform's file, or current_version already ≥ latest.

## To deploy & verify

```
cd ../web && git diff app/api/v1/update/route.ts   # sanity check
# commit/push → Vercel autodeploys
```

Then re-run the curl:
```
curl -i -X POST https://www.zeo.dev/api/v1/update \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"platform":"macos","arch":"unified","current_version":"0.1.1","lang":"en","last_check":""}'
```

**Expected now:** `HTTP/2 200` with `{"version":"0.2.0", …}` since `0.2.0/zeo-0.2.0.dmg` exists in your bucket.

And `current_version=0.2.0` (or higher) should still return 204.

## Side note — not changed but worth flagging

- `web/lib/constants.ts` still exports `ZEO_VERSION = '0.1.1'`. That value is now only used by `app/api/download/route.ts:12` as the default version when the `version` query param is absent. Eventually you may want to drop this default and derive it the same way (or require `version` as a query param) — but that's a separate, optional cleanup.
- `ZEO_RELEASE_DATE` in `lib/constants.ts` is now unused. Safe to delete if you want.
- The vitest config has a pre-existing ESM/CommonJS interop break (`ERR_REQUIRE_ESM` on `vite/dist/node/index.js`). Unrelated to this work, but worth fixing soon so the test suite runs in CI.

content.text:
Server side confirmed working — 200 with the right JSON, all four fields present, and the `version` correctly derived from your storage rather than the stale constant.

Quick recap of where the full chain stands:

| Layer | Status |
|---|---|
| Server: `/api/v1/update` returns 200 with latest version | ✅ Just verified |
| Server: same call on `staging` (no artifacts there) returns 204 | ✅ Earlier verified |
| Client (launcher): manual "Check for Updates…" menu | ✅ In source, needs rebuild |
| Client: visible feedback on 204, network failure, parse error | ✅ In source, needs rebuild |
| Client: `m_working` re-entrancy guard fixed | ✅ In source, needs rebuild |
| Build: `dev/mac_build.sh --release` → `-DZEO_RELEASE=ON` | ✅ Already correct |
| Build: `dev/appimage_build.sh --package --release` → `-DZEO_RELEASE=ON` | ✅ Just added |
| Build: `dev/win_build_installer.ps1 -Release` → `-DZEO_RELEASE=ON` | ✅ Just added |

## What to do next

1. **Rebuild and smoke-test the launcher** end-to-end on macOS:
   ```
   dev/mac_build.sh --release 0.2.1
   WXTRACE=UPDATE ./<path-to>/Zeo.app/Contents/MacOS/Zeo
   ```
   With the current source at 0.2.1, the auto-check on startup will hit the server, the server will return 204 (you're already on the latest), and the trace will say so. Then **Help → Check for Updates…** should pop "You're up to date".

2. **Force the "update available" path** by temporarily editing `src/zeo/cmake/ZeoVersion.cmake` to `0.1.0`, rebuild with `--release 0.1.0`, launch — the auto-check should pop the update dialog on startup. Revert the version after testing. (Don't commit the version downgrade.)

3. **When you ship 0.2.1** (or whatever the next real release is), upload to `zeo-releases/0.2.1/` per existing conventions. No web-side change needed anymore — the route picks it up on the next request.

If you want me to commit any of the C++/build-script changes from this session (they're staged in the working tree but not committed), let me know which scope you'd like in the commit and I'll prepare it.
